title: Psalms 83
subtitle: Do Not Keep Silent, O God

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Deborah and Barak

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “Your Enemies Conspire Against Your People”

heb.paragraph: "With cunning they conspire (sōd) against your people" (v.3). Ten nations form an alliance against Israel (vv.6–8): Edom, Ishmaelites, Moab, Hagrites, Byblos, Ammon, Amalek, Philistia, Tyre, Assyria. They say: "Come, let us destroy them as a nation" (v.4). The last Asaph psalm.

notes.note: "Do not keep silent, O God! See how your enemies growl. They say, ‘Come, let us destroy them as a nation.’" The conspiracy targets Israel’s existence. Not conquest but annihilation.
Ten nations listed: the comprehensive coalition. The alliance includes traditional enemies from every direction.

refs.note: Jehoshaphat's prayer when surrounded by Moab, Ammon, and Edom faces a similar coalition.
The defeat of Midian and the killing of Oreb and Zeeb are cited as models for hoped-for victory.
The great supper of God with birds eating the flesh of kings fulfills the psalm's judgment imagery.

hist.context: Psalm 83 catalogs a ten-nation coalition against Israel: Edom, Ishmaelites, Moab, Hagrites, Gebal, Ammon, Amalek, Philistia, Tyre, and Assyria. No historical situation matches this exact combination, leading some scholars to see it as composite or representative of all enemies. The detailed list emphasizes total encirclement by hostile neighbors.

sections.header: Verses 9–18 — “Let Them Know That You Alone Are the Most High”

heb.paragraph: "Let them know that you, whose name is the LORD — that you alone are the Most High (ʿeləyōn) over all the earth" (v.18). Asaph asks God to treat these enemies like Midian, Sisera, and Jabin (v.9) — defeated in Judges. Make them like tumbleweed, like fire that burns the forest (vv.13–14). The purpose: "so that they will seek your name, LORD" (v.16). The goal is not annihilation but recognition.

notes.note: "Cover their faces with shame, LORD, so that they will seek your name. Let them know that you alone are the Most High over all the earth." The surprising purpose of judgment: that the nations would SEEK GOD. Defeat leads to recognition.

refs.note: God magnifying himself so that nations will know he is the LORD echoes the psalm's purpose for judgment.
Every tongue confessing the name above all names fulfills the psalm's vision of nations acknowledging YHWH.
All nations worshiping because God's righteous acts are revealed completes the psalm's goal.

hist.context: The psalm requests enemies become 'like tumbleweed' and 'chaff' (v.13), driven by divine wind. The purpose is not merely national survival but theological witness: 'let them know that you, whose name is the LORD, are the Most High over all the earth' (v.18). Judgment serves revelation — through defeated enemies, all nations learn YHWH's sovereignty.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "Do not keep silent, O God! See how your enemies growl…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVSee how your enemies growl, how your foes rear their heads.ESVFor behold, your enemies make an uproar; those who hate you have raised their heads.NIVEven Assyria has joined them to reinforce Lot’s descendants.ESVAsshur also has joined them; they are the strong arm of the children of Lot. Selah

rec.text: ""Do not keep silent, O God! See how your enemies growl. They say, ‘Come, let us destroy them as a nation.’" The conspiracy targets Israel’s existence. Not conquest but annihilation."

rows.label: The Coalition — 83:1–8
rows.text: Ten nations: "Let us destroy them as a nation." Conspiracy against God’s people.

hebtext.note: "With cunning they conspire (sōd) against your people" (v.3). Ten nations form an alliance against Israel (vv.6–8): Edom, Ishmaelites, Moab, Hagrites, Byblos, Ammon, Amalek, Philistia, Tyre, Assyria. They say: "Come, let us destroy them as a nation" (v.4). The last Asaph psalm.

"Let them know that you, whose name is the LORD — that you alone are the Most High (ʿeləyōn) over all the earth" (v.18). Asaph asks God to treat these enemies like Midian, Sisera, and Jabin (v.9) — defeated in Judges. Make them like tumbleweed, like fire that burns the forest (vv.13–14). The purpose: "so that they will seek your name, LORD" (v.16). The goal is not annihilation but recognition.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks: the Most High over all the earth. Mission: the surprising purpose — "that they will seek your name."
